papers.title: Automated Big Text Security Classification
papers.abstract: In recent years, traditional cybersecurity safeguards have proven ineffective against insider threats. Famous cases of sensitive information leaks caused by insiders, including the WikiLeaks release of diplomatic cables and the Edward Snowden incident, have greatly harmed the U.S. government's relationship with other governments and with its own citizens. Data Leak Prevention (DLP) is a solution for detecting and preventing information leaks from within an organization's network. However, state-of-art DLP detection models are only able to detect very limited types of sensitive information, and research in the field has been hindered due to the lack of available sensitive texts. Many researchers have focused on document-based detection with artificially labeled "confidential documents" for which security labels are assigned to the entire document, when in reality only a portion of the document is sensitive. This type of whole-document based security labeling increases the chances of preventing authorized users from accessing non-sensitive information within sensitive documents. In this paper, we introduce Automated Classification Enabled by Security Similarity (ACESS), a new and innovative detection model that penetrates the complexity of big text security classification/detection. To analyze the ACESS system, we constructed a novel dataset, containing formerly classified paragraphs from diplomatic cables made public by the WikiLeaks organization. To our knowledge this paper is the first to analyze a dataset that contains actual formerly sensitive information annotated at paragraph granularity.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's research idea focuses on network intrusion detection using hyperbolic embedding for network flow relationships and hierarchical structures. The paper, however, addresses 'Automated Big Text Security Classification' and 'Data Leak Prevention (DLP)' by classifying sensitive text at a paragraph level, which is a distinct problem from network intrusion detection, even though both relate to cybersecurity. | Method: The user's research idea proposes a network intrusion detection system using hyperbolic embedding spaces to model hierarchical network structures. The evaluated paper, 'Automated Big Text Security Classification,' introduces ACESS, a system for classifying sensitive text at a paragraph level using clustering and similarity-based classification models. There is no mention of hyperbolic geometry, network intrusion detection, or network flow relationships in the paper, indicating completely different methodologies. | Domain: The user's research idea focuses on a network intrusion detection system, which falls under cybersecurity. The paper, 'Automated Big Text Security Classification,' also addresses a cybersecurity problem: data leak prevention (DLP) and sensitive text classification within an organization's network. While both are in cybersecurity, the paper's specific application is text classification for insider threat detection, whereas the user's idea is about network flow analysis using hyperbolic embeddings for intrusion detection, representing different sub-areas and application targets within the broader cybersecurity domain. | Contribution: The user's research idea proposes a network intrusion detection system using hyperbolic embedding for network flow relationships and hierarchical structures. In contrast, the paper focuses on 'Automated Big Text Security Classification' for Data Leak Prevention (DLP) by classifying sensitive text at a paragraph level using similarity-based models and clustering. There is no overlap in the proposed methods (hyperbolic embedding vs. text classification) or the specific application domains (network intrusion detection vs. insider threat text leaks).

papers.title: Hyperbolic Geometry in Computer Vision: A Survey
papers.abstract: Hyperbolic geometry, a Riemannian manifold endowed with constant sectional negative curvature, has been considered an alternative embedding space in many learning scenarios, \eg, natural language processing, graph learning, \etc, as a result of its intriguing property of encoding the data's hierarchical structure (like irregular graph or tree-likeness data). Recent studies prove that such data hierarchy also exists in the visual dataset, and investigate the successful practice of hyperbolic geometry in the computer vision (CV) regime, ranging from the classical image classification to advanced model adaptation learning. This paper presents the first and most up-to-date literature review of hyperbolic spaces for CV applications. To this end, we first introduce the background of hyperbolic geometry, followed by a comprehensive investigation of algorithms, with geometric prior of hyperbolic space, in the context of visual applications. We also conclude this manuscript and identify possible future directions.

The evaluated paper is a survey on hyperbolic geometry in computer vision applications, focusing on image classification, generation, and segmentation. While both involve hyperbolic geometry, the application domains (network intrusion detection vs. computer vision) and the specific methods (network flow analysis vs. image processing) are entirely different, indicating no methodological overlap. | Domain: The user's research idea is in network intrusion detection, which falls under network security or computer networks. The paper focuses on hyperbolic geometry in computer vision applications, such as image classification, generation, and segmentation. The user's idea, however, specifically proposes using hyperbolic embedding for network intrusion detection to model hierarchical network topology and attack taxonomies, which is a distinct application domain from the CV focus of the paper.

papers.title: The Everyday Security of Living with Conflict
papers.abstract: When `cyber' is used as a prefix, attention is typically drawn to the technological and spectacular aspects of war and conflict -- and, by extension, security. We offer a different approach to engaging with and understanding security in such contexts, by foregrounding the everyday -- mundane -- experiences of security within communities living with and fleeing from war. We do so through three vignettes from our field research in Colombia, Lebanon and Sweden, respectively, and by highlighting the significance of ethnography for security research with communities living in regions afflicted by war. We conclude by setting out a call to action for security researchers and practitioners to consider such lived experiences in the design of security technology that aims to cater to the needs of communities in `global conflict and disaster regions'.

These are fundamentally different research problems. | Method: The user's research idea proposes a technical method involving hyperbolic embedding spaces for network intrusion detection. The paper, in contrast, focuses on ethnographic research and qualitative methodologies to understand the everyday experiences of security in conflict regions, explicitly calling for a broader conception of security beyond technological questions. There is no methodological overlap between these two approaches. | Domain: The user's research idea is focused on network intrusion detection systems, a technical cybersecurity domain. papers.title: Hyperbolic geometry and non-Kahler manifolds with trivial canonical bundle
papers.abstract: We use hyperbolic geometry to construct simply-connected symplectic or complex manifolds with trivial canonical bundle and with no compatible Kahler structure. We start with the desingularisations of the quadric cone in C^4: the smoothing is a natural S^3-bundle over H^3, its holomorphic geometry is determined by the hyperbolic metric; the small-resolution is a natural S^2-bundle over H^4 with symplectic geometry determined by the metric. Using hyperbolic geometry, we find orbifold quotients with trivial canonical bundle; smooth examples are produced via crepant resolutions. In particular, we find the first example of a simply-connected symplectic 6-manifold with c_1=0 that does not admit a compatible Kahler structure. We also find infinitely many distinct complex structures on 2(S^3xS^3)#(S^2xS^4) with trivial canonical bundle. Finally, we explain how an analogous construction for hyperbolic manifolds in higher dimensions gives symplectic non-Kahler "Fano" manifolds of dimension 12 and higher.

papers.title: Automated Big Text Security Classification
papers.abstract: In recent years, traditional cybersecurity safeguards have proven ineffective against insider threats. Famous cases of sensitive information leaks caused by insiders, including the WikiLeaks release of diplomatic cables and the Edward Snowden incident, have greatly harmed the U.S. government's relationship with other governments and with its own citizens. Data Leak Prevention (DLP) is a solution for detecting and preventing information leaks from within an organization's network. However, state-of-art DLP detection models are only able to detect very limited types of sensitive information, and research in the field has been hindered due to the lack of available sensitive texts. Many researchers have focused on document-based detection with artificially labeled "confidential documents" for which security labels are assigned to the entire document, when in reality only a portion of the document is sensitive. This type of whole-document based security labeling increases the chances of preventing authorized users from accessing non-sensitive information within sensitive documents. In this paper, we introduce Automated Classification Enabled by Security Similarity (ACESS), a new and innovative detection model that penetrates the complexity of big text security classification/detection. To analyze the ACESS system, we constructed a novel dataset, containing formerly classified paragraphs from diplomatic cables made public by the WikiLeaks organization. To our knowledge this paper is the first to analyze a dataset that contains actual formerly sensitive information annotated at paragraph granularity.

existing_examples.name: Traditional Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS)
existing_examples.description: Existing NIDS (e.g., Snort, Suricata) detect intrusions but typically use rule-based, signature-based, or statistical/machine learning methods in Euclidean spaces, not explicitly hyperbolic embeddings for network flow relationships.

existing_examples.name: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for Network Security
existing_examples.description: Research exists on using GNNs to model network traffic as graphs and detect anomalies. Some GNNs can implicitly learn hierarchical features, but the explicit use of hyperbolic embeddings for *network flow relationships* in an NIDS is less common.

existing_examples.name: Hyperbolic Embeddings in Machine Learning
existing_examples.description: Hyperbolic embeddings are a known research area, particularly for modeling hierarchical data (e.g., knowledge graphs, word hierarchies, social networks). Applying this specific technique to *network flow relationships* within an NIDS is the novel aspect.

existing_examples.name: Hierarchical Clustering/Modeling in Network Security
existing_examples.description: Network security often deals with hierarchical data (e.g., IP address ranges, attack trees, protocol stacks). Traditional methods exist for modeling these, but not typically through explicit hyperbolic geometry for *flow relationships*.

reality_check.assessment: While Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) are a well-established field, and hyperbolic embeddings are a known technique in machine learning for hierarchical data, the specific combination of using a *hyperbolic embedding space to represent network flow relationships* for an NIDS appears to be a novel research direction. Existing NIDS use various techniques, and while some might implicitly handle hierarchical data, the explicit leveraging of hyperbolic geometry for this purpose, especially for *flow relationships* and *attack taxonomies*, is not a widely adopted or commercialized approach in NIDS products. There is research in both areas, but their direct integration as described is less common.
novelty_aspects: Explicit application of hyperbolic embedding space to model *network flow relationships* within an NIDS.
Leveraging hyperbolic geometry's ability to model hierarchical structures specifically for *network topology and attack taxonomies* in an NIDS context.
Potential for more efficient and accurate representation of complex, hierarchical network data compared to Euclidean methods.
reality_check.recommendation: This idea shows significant novelty in its specific application of hyperbolic embeddings to network flow relationships for intrusion detection. Focus on demonstrating the practical advantages (e.g., improved detection rates, reduced false positives, better interpretability of hierarchical attacks) over existing NIDS and machine learning-based security systems that operate in Euclidean spaces. Research existing academic papers that might touch upon similar concepts to ensure full originality.

matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: In recent years, traditional cybersecurity safeguards have proven ineffective against insider threats. Famous cases of sensitive information leaks caused by insiders, including the WikiLeaks release of diplomatic cables and the Edward Snowden incident, have greatly harmed the U.S. government's relationship with other governments and with its own citizens. Data Leak Prevention (DLP) is a solution for detecting and preventing information leaks from within an organization's network.
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper operate within the broad domain of cybersecurity, specifically focusing on detection systems within a network context. The paper discusses Data Leak Prevention (DLP) for detecting information leaks from within an organization's network, which is a related but distinct application area from network intrusion detection.

matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: Sensitive information leakage occurs when data passes from trusted to untrusted channels. Leading cybersecurity vendors have started to adopt DLP [4] [5] to counter the problem via a detection model that differentiates sensitive information from non-sensitive information.
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper are situated in the cybersecurity domain, with the paper focusing on Data Leak Prevention (DLP) within networks, which shares the general field of securing network-related activities, even if the specific problem and method differ.

linked_sections.paper_title: Hyperbolic Geometry in Computer Vision: A Survey
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Hyperbolic geometry, a Riemannian manifold endowed with constant sectional negative curvature, has been considered an alternative embedding space in many learning scenarios, e.g., natural language processing, graph learning, etc., as a result of its intriguing property of encoding the data's hierarchical structure (like irregular graph or tree-likeness data).
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper discuss hyperbolic geometry as an embedding space for modeling hierarchical structures. The user applies this to network flow relationships and attack taxonomies, which are specific instances of hierarchical data that the paper generally refers to as 'irregular graph or tree-likeness data'.
